speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Moses Kaufman. This is tape three. Moses, you were talking about just when the Germans occupied Vilna. Did you have any warning of what was about to occur?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When that happens-- actually, I would like to go back a little bit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember it quite rightly. When I was on the border with the Russia, in that little town, Korzec--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is at the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is on the beginning of the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I notice that the army, the Russian army, and especially the one what were in the big trucks, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there have been colossal posters were showing from one side, we know-- and that was a known fact-- that Russia was with Germany, a written-- have got a written [NON-ENGLISH]. I mentioned that to you before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] between Ribbentrop and that Molotov. And when I showed the trucks, the big trucks that were some big posters-- were SS, German, with their [NON-ENGLISH] [INAUDIBLE], blood's running from his mouth. And a word here, keeping the standard. And with the cycle-- cycle and helmet, and this. I couldn't combine the two things. On one side is written [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] between the two countries, between Russia and, and Germany. And here, again, she poses like this. I couldn't understand this. But I came to-- so actually, I was thinking of this, that something is fishy in there. Something is-- will happen. And the question, what you gave me now, is telling me that I was right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That I was thinking about this, but I couldn't believe it. I couldn't believe it, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when did the Germans occupy Vilna? How soon after they declared war with Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came into Vilna through Klaipeda. That was a port. And that happens on the 20-- no, when did they? They invaded Lithuania on the 23rd, 23rd, of June. And they came in about two days later. They came into Vilna already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember that day, when the Germans came into Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember this quite rightly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want, for instance, to work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was on a Monday. There were two lieutenants, one of the name Habik. And the other's name was Lavatchek.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- which--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were assigned [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as the people [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from the [INAUDIBLE] east. And they-- that's in [INAUDIBLE], was in Riga. And they ask everything was [INAUDIBLE]. They [NON-ENGLISH]. They took over. That combined. The [INAUDIBLE] that combined the [SPEAKING GERMAN] in Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't went to work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They ask-- that was Habik or Lavatchek-- the people who was the technical manager here? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they find out that a gentleman, Kaufman-- was-- Before you go on, could you just spell their names for me please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Habik-- H-A-B-I-K-- Habik. And the other's was Lavatchek-- L-A-V-A-T-C-H-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what unit of the German military they were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what unit of the German military force they were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was in the-- in the general Wehrmacht. Wehrmact, the Wehrmacht took over. Actually, the Wehrmacht took over that combine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they found out that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was. And they're sending under officer. And the name, because he was-- I was with-- and the name was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Martin Hanze.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-R-T-I-N, Martin, the first name. Hanze-- H-A-N-Z-E. Hanze. Hanze. That was his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came. They asked-- they find out that-- Habik find out where I'm living from the people there in the factory. He came to me. Now, I will say this in German, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came in. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, yes. I am Kaufman. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, to him, yes, I was the one. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: I accent this, Herr Kaufman. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mister. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SPEAKING GERMAN] And I went to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So could you just translate what you just said, the last sentence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Be so good, Mister Kaufman. The lieutenant, Habik and Lieutenant Lavatchek want to talk to you. And I went, and the same thing, that Habik said to me. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That the army took over that battery-- the-- no, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: They combine the factories. That what I can't forget too, that what they told me. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to do this, Mr. Kaufman. You have to do this further, to do in the capacity as a technical manager. And he add to it that [GERMAN], that I will have to eat. [GERMAN]-- you know? Provisions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Provisions. That I will-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] That there will be for me. And I was carrying on for them so as I was carrying on for the army, for the Red Army as a technical manager. Because that combine, when there was a national list, that all products was going to the Red Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and that-- and this-- and I start to work for the-- for the-- for the-- for, for the German army [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] as a technical manager. The factor-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because that first, when the-- when they-- when they asked me to do that, already Lithuanians were taking over positions. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one engineer, [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He-- they nominate he was the technical manager. But he hasn't got the slightest idea about tanning, about carrying on a tannery. So de facto, I was the one waiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did life change in Vilna once the Germans occupied?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] starts straight away from the beginning, a horrible time. That was a hell, a horrible time for the Jews. And that wasn't done-- that was still before the Gestapo or the SS or the SD came to the town. The Lithuanians, they want-- they called them-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] white arm, arm [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bands. They-- they call-- they were the Nationalists. They were the killers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning there were some uniforms, too, from the police, from the army, from the ghetto army. But most of them, they were in civil, carrying white, white bands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Arm band.

speaker: interviewee
text: Arm bands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the white arm band have any writing insignia on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. I, I left-- I believe-- I don't remember that quite rightly. I can't remember that quite rightly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what occurred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Straight away, killings of Jews. They were-- they were killing. They were assembling them in the [NON-ENGLISH], in the [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] They were the one before the [GERMAN] came to Vilna. And they were the one. They were the killers. They were the one. They were-- they called them harpoonists. And they were the one, that they were doing the-- all, all, all killings of the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Killings? Yes. When the ghetto-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but that would-- most probably, I will tell you. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] Instead to go to the ghetto, because the ghetto was full-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] so the unlucky people, they were herded in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] jails. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were horrible things there done, really. I witness, for instance, in the cell, where [INAUDIBLE] 30 or 40 people, there was about 200 or 250 people there. And one lady, elderly lady, took her life by taking some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] drugs. I don't know what that was. Because you ask me if I was close to it. So when they start to knock through the door that a person is dying, and so on, they came in. That was that [GERMAN]. And they said like this-- now [SPEAKING GERMAN] And he took out from-- a revolver and give it [INAUDIBLE] and kill her.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the lady who took the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was about-- that was about five meters. Because everyone was sitting on [INAUDIBLE] and so on. And I was-- I was a witness to that, what happened that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you end up in this jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was on the sixth. Was it? [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Don't remember the date. The date when the ghetto was established. And instead to go to the ghetto-- and that Hanze, that Martin Hanze, he was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] went, also, to the police what was driving us to the ghetto. Remember to the ghetto, remember to the ghetto. And the next day, when I didn't turn, that was on a Saturday. This was on a Saturday. Yes, and Monday, I didn't came to work. So they went to look after me in the ghetto. In the ghetto they find out that I am not there, that he most-- that I am most probably in the in, in [INAUDIBLE] in that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: In that jail. They was looking for me. He told me after, when he find me, that was the last cell. That was a colossal jail. That he look after me. Kaufman! He was calling, Kaufman! Kaufman! And I said, here! [GERMAN] And I took my wife. And I took, still, Mr. Fabykant, that he survive, and his-- because he was working also, on the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell his name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fabykant. F-A-B-Y-K-A-N-T. Fabykant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you took them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Took them. And the-- the brother was also trying-- also-- the brother, [? Moishe ?] Fabykant. He was also with his wife. And the daughter. Fabykant, the wife, and the daughter was-- I took them with me. You know? And the door was locked up. And the brother with the wife and family stayed. A few hours later, the-- all people there went to Ponary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were killed there. That was the place where the armbands, the Lithuanians, tens of thousands of people in Lithuania were killed through them. And in Vilna, every second day, every three days, 200, 250, 300 people, Ponary. They were the killers. Before, the-- the original Gestapos and SD came into, to, to Vilna, they were the killers. They were the organizers. They were the killers. They were our killers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Hanze came and took you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Took me out. And he told us, told my wife and me, [INAUDIBLE], that was the last cell, that he was looking after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: He engaged two Lithuanians, that all our packs were loaded on that two-wheel, little-- what do you call it? Cart?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cart. And he was pressed. He brought us in, in, in, into the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Lithuanians were the one-- the two, it took two Lithuanians to bring it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the ghetto for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was horrible. It was a horrible thing there. Because it was full. It was closed. It was a lot of people in there. To describe, what I would describe, in a room like this one here, that was 16 people. So at night we were living like sardines, on the-- on the-- on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the boundaries of the-- of the ghetto? How was it confined? Was there-- were there walls? Was there barbed wire, fences? How was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [INAUDIBLE], there's a part of the Jewish quarters have been-- but it wasn't enough for so many people living. Don't forget, that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Vilna and all little towns around, little villages, little towns, it's over, over, I believe, 90,000 100,000 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people there, living there. And this small place was only allocated for that ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you spend in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was spending the year till, till-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] it was September, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you working in the tannery while you were still in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the tannery. I was working in tannery. And some people were working in the tannery, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it the same tannery that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was. Yes, I was-- as-- the factory as a technical manager in the tannery for the Wehrmacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the tannery within the boundaries of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The tannery was not. It was outside. It was-- all tanneries that have been on the-- nearly on the river Vilnia, lying one after the other. And they make from them, one big combine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to work from the ghetto to the tannery?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the ghetto to the tannery, we were going in so-called [NON-ENGLISH]. People not singly, but in parties. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And coming back, also, in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and who guarded you? Who watched over you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were guarded through the-- through the Jewish police, there, what was builded, was formed there. Some through Germans that need them for their [GERMAN]. They need the work done for them. So a German was cutting. And we were going without any-- without any-- what do they call it? Guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the thought of escape every occur to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning-- in the beginning no. In the beginning, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody try to escape when they were being marched to the tannery?

speaker: interviewee
text: In march to the tannery, no. There wasn't-- there wasn't that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you obtain food within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Having the contact with the outside, or we were buying the food and trying to bring it in, in ghetto. But before going in, before the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, before the entrance to the ghetto, always we were-- horrible things were happening. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because some Lithuanians from the police and so on, they were looking for some food in everyone's-- so I myself, I got ones from one young German on my face. So I-- horrible. In fact, I got some bread brought. You find some bread and you know? I escaped that time. What do you mean, you escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: I escaped inside. You know? That was so quickly. He hit me once. He hit me twice. And after that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Judenrat organized in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a Judenrat. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember the names quite rightly. You know? I don't remember the names quite rightly. And after the head of the ghetto, that was [? Gentz. ?] He was a captain and a Jew, a captain in the-- in the Lithuanian army. [SIGHS] There was another one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The police was carrying on with the-- there was-- Judenrat was in the beginning-- so-- in beginning. And after, the police took over. And that [? Gentz ?] took over with other, other commandants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware that there were two ghettos in Vilna at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware that there were two ghettos in Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the ghetto number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the difference between the two ghettos? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selections, they had been in the number one, and the number one through. And sending, then, we went to number two. And after, to Ponary. Selections. [SPEAKING GERMAN] Something they find out, some other things, the big selections. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it known by the people in ghetto one of your fate if you went to ghetto two?

speaker: interviewee
text: The fight?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people know that if they were being selected to go to ghetto two, what would happen to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: That happens every-- no. We didn't know about this. But it happens. So we know about this, that it's going to make selections. They want whatever's there, right and [INAUDIBLE] or-- and one went to Ponary. The others went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of your position in the tannery, were you exempt [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from these selections? Or did you survive by luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: First-- no. No, I wouldn't say that. I was actually-- it's very-- I got a schein. So long as I was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's a schein?

speaker: interviewee
text: A schein, that is papers that I'm working for the-- in the tanneries. And, you know, that the products produced there, they are very essential in time for the war. So they were-- they were trying to have the people that they will-- It's hard-- For instance, somebody telled to-- tell me, all right, the Germans did out a good-- a good job because they were trying to give you work and to, to, to have this supply, you know, of-- to, to feed you and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from the other side, I'm thinking that I believe that they were hit-- watching themselves, not to be sent to the East Front. You know? And better to be occupied. And they were looking for themself, I believe, more than for the-- for the Jews. No one was good to us. So you had the schein, the work permit, which allowed you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To actually-- that I was not selected to go to Ponary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking about the times in Vilna ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Ala, your wife, doing at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Working also, in the tannery. Going in the [NON-ENGLISH], and going to work to-- so decide to work in the tanneries.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a break here, and just change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.
